lesson.title: Confidence Intervals
lesson.body: A 95% confidence interval is a random interval constructed from the data such that, in repeated sampling, 95% of such intervals contain the true parameter. For μ with known σ, the CI is X̄ ± z_{α/2}·σ/√n. With unknown σ, replace z with the t-distribution critical value. The width is controlled by the margin of error z·σ/√n.

rooms.question: What is the correct interpretation of a 95% confidence interval?
answers: If we repeated sampling many times, 95% of such intervals would contain μ
There is a 95% probability that μ lies in this specific interval
95% of the data falls in this interval
The true μ is within the interval with certainty
rooms.explanation: The confidence level is a frequentist property of the procedure, not a probability statement about the specific realized interval. μ is fixed; the interval is random.

rooms.question: For a 95% CI on μ with known σ, the critical value z_{0.025} is approximately:
rooms.explanation: For 95% CI, α = 0.05, α/2 = 0.025, and P(Z ≤ 1.96) ≈ 0.975 in standard normal tables.

rooms.question: To halve the margin of error in a CI while keeping confidence level fixed, you must:
steps: \text{To halve ME, need } \sqrt{n} \text{ to double, so } n \text{ must quadruple}
rooms.explanation: Since ME ∝ 1/√n, halving ME requires multiplying n by 4. The margin of error decreases slowly with sample size.

rooms.question: When σ is unknown, the 95% CI for μ uses the t-distribution with n−1 degrees of freedom. For large n, t_{n-1, 0.025} approaches:
rooms.explanation: As degrees of freedom → ∞, the t-distribution converges to the standard normal, so t_{n-1,0.025} → z_{0.025} = 1.96.

rooms.question: Order the steps to construct a t-confidence interval for μ:
items: Compute X̄ and S from the sample
Determine degrees of freedom df = n − 1
Find t* = t_{df, α/2} from t-table
rooms.explanation: Compute sample statistics, set degrees of freedom, find the critical value, then construct the interval.

boss.name: The Interval Inquisitor
boss.taunt: Tell me where the truth lies. You have only finite data and infinite uncertainty.

questions.question: A 95% CI for μ is (12.3, 18.7). A 99% CI based on the same data will be:
questions.explanation: Higher confidence → larger critical value z_{α/2} → wider interval. The 99% CI captures more uncertainty.

questions.question: For a one-sided 95% upper CI, the critical value from N(0,1) is:
questions.explanation: One-sided 95% CI puts all α=0.05 in one tail: use z_{0.05} ≈ 1.645, not z_{0.025} ≈ 1.96 which is for two-sided.

questions.question: The width of a CI for proportion p (using normal approximation) is maximized when:
questions.explanation: p(1-p) is maximized at p=0.5, giving the widest CI. This is why conservative sample size calculations assume p=0.5.
